sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 65-year-old female, presented for follow-up after an inferior STEMI where a stent was placed.
She reports experiencing similar symptoms to her previous heart attack, including chest pain that would not go away, prompting her to visit the emergency room.
She denies any chest pain or shortness of breath since her last visit six months ago and has been able to exercise by walking her dog daily.
She has a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and diabetes.
She has noticed swelling in her legs since being discharged from the hospital.
She is compliant with her medications and has been taking her prescribed medications as directed.
She has not been watching her salt intake, particularly enjoying pizza and pepperoni as her favorite foods.
She has been managing her diabetes with metformin and walking her dog to stay active.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and diabetes.
Review of systems: The patient denies any chest pain or shortness of breath since her last visit six months ago.
She has noticed swelling in her legs since being discharged from the hospital.
Current medications:
Aspirin 81 mg daily
Brilinta 90 mg twice daily
Lipitor 80 mg daily
Toprol 50 mg daily
Metformin (for diabetes)
Lisinopril 20 mg daily
Objective:
Vital signs: Not explicitly mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck exam was unremarkable, with no jugular venous distention or carotid bruits.
The heart exam revealed a 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base.
The lungs were clear.
The extremities showed lower limb edema, with a 1+ pitting edema on the right radial artery.
The cath site was clean, dry, and intact without hematoma, and the right radial artery pulses were palpable.
Test Results:
ECG: Normal sinus rhythm, good R wave progression, and expected changes after a STEMI.
Echo: Reduced ejection fraction of 35%, moderate mitral regurgitation.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has coronary artery disease, newly reduced left ventricular dysfunction, and moderate mitral regurgitation, with a current episode of acute heart failure.
Problem list: Coronary artery disease; Reduced left ventricular dysfunction; Moderate mitral regurgitation; Acute heart failure; Hypertension.
Plan:
I will continue the patient on Aspirin 81 mg daily, Brilinta 90 mg twice daily, Lipitor 80 mg daily, and Toprol 50 mg daily.
I will refer her to cardiac rehab for education and support.
I will prescribe Lasix 40 mg daily and Aldactone 12.5 mg daily to manage fluid retention.
I will also repeat an echo in two months.
I will refer her to nutrition counseling to discuss dietary changes, including reducing salt intake and exploring alternative protein sources.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in two months to monitor her progress and adjust her treatment plan as needed.
